title: RESTRICT-by-Default Delete Taxonomy
context: Entities have relationships. Deleting an entity must handle its dependents. Without a clear taxonomy, each feature invents its own delete behaviour — some cascade silently, some fail cryptically, some orphan data.
decision: **Four-category delete taxonomy with RESTRICT as the default.**
| Category | Behaviour | When | Example |

| **SOFT DELETE** | Set `IsDeleted=true`, retain record | Entities requiring regulatory retention, audit trail preservation, restore semantics, or versioned configuration history | ActionEntity, ActionEvidence, ActionConsultation, versioned configuration rows where implemented |
| **CASCADE** | Delete children with parent | Owned children within same aggregate | ComplianceProfile child rules when deleting an inactive profile |
| **RESTRICT** | Block with 422 + blocker details | Cross-aggregate references | WorkflowDefinition with active Actions |
| **SET NULL** | Null the FK, keep the record | Severable/informational references | Optional FK where parent deletion is allowed |
**RESTRICT is the default for hard deletes.** Every FK must have an explicit `OnDelete` in the EF configuration (see ADR-0018). No implicit cascade behaviour.

**Soft delete applies to entities implementing `ISoftDeletable` or module-specific `IsDeleted` retention semantics.** These entities are never physically deleted by normal admin delete commands. Delete commands set `IsDeleted=true` plus audit/concurrency fields when available. EF global query filters (`HasQueryFilter(e => !e.IsDeleted)`) exclude soft-deleted records where the entity participates in the shared soft-delete pattern. Use `IgnoreQueryFilters()` when soft-deleted records must be included (audit, admin restore).
**Why soft delete for these entities:**
- **Regulatory retention** - ISO 45001, MSHA, OSHA, EPA require action records and evidence to be retained for compliance periods (up to 6 years for BSEE SEMS evidence)
- **GDPR Article 17 compliance** - soft delete satisfies the "right to erasure" for application visibility while retaining data for regulatory obligations under Article 17(3)(b)
- **Audit trail integrity** - ActionAuditEvent and ActionStateHistory reference ActionEntity IDs; hard delete would orphan these records
- **Cross-product references** - source links from Capstone/Guardian/Pulse point to ActionEntity; hard delete would break external references

- **Configuration restore/version history** - versioned admin configuration such as escalation rules and deadline types can be hidden from normal use without losing historical concurrency/audit context
**All other entities use hard delete** with the CASCADE/RESTRICT/SET NULL taxonomy below.

**API contract:** Admin delete endpoints must carry optimistic concurrency data. Collection-level deletes use `DELETE /api/v1/admin/{entities}` with `Items[]` (`Id` + `RowVersion`), with `Version` allowed only as a documented compatibility fallback for versioned configuration rows. Single-aggregate deletes use `DELETE /api/v1/admin/{entities}/{id}` with a request body containing `RowVersion` when the operation is inherently one-record. Both are allowed variants; this taxonomy governs what the command may remove, not which route shape is selected.
**RESTRICT response:** HTTP 422 with structured error body listing what is blocking deletion. The caller gets actionable information ("3 actions reference this workflow - reassign them first").
**Delete command pattern (hard delete):**
1. Protection check (system entities, immutable records) -> `CommandResult.Fail(...)` (see ADR-0010)
2. Concurrency check using `RowVersion` or a documented `Version` fallback -> `CommandResult.Fail(..., 409)`
3. RESTRICT pre-check - query dependent records, return blocker details if any exist
4. Begin transaction when the command owns the write session
5. SET NULL updates (if applicable)
6. CASCADE/delete owned children (if applicable)

7. Delete the root entity. Use `ExecuteDeleteAsync` for simple set-based deletes; load the aggregate and use `Remove`/`RemoveRange` when row-version checks, child cleanup, audit details, or domain rules require loaded state.
8. Commit
9. Post-commit cleanup (cache invalidation, blob deletion)
**Delete command pattern (soft delete):**
1. Protection check -> `CommandResult.Fail(...)`
2. Concurrency check using `RowVersion` or a documented `Version` fallback -> `CommandResult.Fail(..., 409)`
3. Load entity (required to set fields)

4. Set `IsDeleted = true` and update audit/concurrency fields (`DeletedDate`/`DeletedById` where the entity implements the shared soft-delete interface, otherwise `UpdatedAt`/`UpdatedBy`/`Version`/`RowVersion`)
5. `SaveChangesAsync` (audit interceptor or command-owned `IAuditService` records the change)
6. Post-save side effects (cache invalidation)
consequences: - Explicit delete behaviour on every FK - no surprises
- Actionable 422 errors - users know what to fix
- Protection checks prevent accidental deletion of system/seed entities
- Admin batch delete support carries per-row concurrency data through `Items[]` (`Id` + `RowVersion`, with documented `Version` fallback only where implemented)
- Single aggregate deletes keep the route id and row-version body contract explicit
- Delete commands are more complex than a simple `Remove()` + `SaveChanges()`
- RESTRICT pre-checks add a query per dependent entity type
- Some configuration modules carry both `Version` and `RowVersion` during a compatibility period, which must stay documented until removed
**Trade-off accepted:** Delete safety and concurrency correctness are worth the query overhead. Silent cascades, stale deletes, and cryptic FK violation errors are worse than explicit pre-checks.
origin: Identity convention (Identity ADR-0022), adopted by Actions for the same reasons.
